significant as the only such example found on Honshu. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The kofun is believed to have been built in the mid-[[6th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The site was Historic site as part of a National [[Historic site]] in [[1999]] (Heisei 11) under the "Mukouyama Kofun Group," and its stone horse was Historic site a National [[Important designation1]] in [[1959]] (Showa 34). == Chronology == * [[1902]] (Meiji 35): [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]] identified the stone horse at Tenjingaki Shrine. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} * During the [[Taisho period]] (1912–1926): Stone fragments were collected near the front part of the tumulus, and the stone horse at Tenjingaki Shrine was confirmed to have originated from this kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} * [[1959]] (Showa 34): The stone horse was Historic site a National [[Important designation1]]. * [[1989]] (Heisei 1): The site underwent trial excavation by the Yodoe Town Board of Education, and a report was published in [[1990]]. * [[1999]] (Heisei 11): The site was Historic site as part of the National [[Historic site]] "Mukouyama Kofun Group." {{National Historic site Database|401|2110|Mukouyama Kofun Group}} == Kofun == The area of the tumulus are as follows: {{PDFlink|[http://yonagobunka.net/system/site/upload/live/12551/atc_1522851322.pdf Yonago City Archaeological Center Newsletter No. 28]}} (Yonago City Archaeological Center, 2018). * Total length: 61.2 meters * Rear circle diameter: 34.5 meters * Rear circle height: 5 meters The tumulus is the second-largest in the Yodoe area, following Mukouyama No. 4 Kofun (64.5 meters). == burial goods == [[File:Ishiumadani Kofun Stone Horse (Replica).JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone horse (replica)}}{{small|Exhibited at the Hakuho Hill Museum. The original is a National [[Important designation1]].}}]] The most notable artifact from Ishiumadani Kofun is the '''stone horse'''. It is a stone representation of a haniwa horse and part of the broader Category:kk]]" (seijin-seiba). The stone, identified as hornblende [[andesite]] from Mount Daisen, measures approximately 150 cm in length and 90 cm in height. Intypeational plaque at Tenjingaki Shrine (Tottori Prefecture Board of Education, 2015). Although the front legs are missing, the surface shows detailed carvings of tack such as bridles, reins, and a sa11le. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The stone horse is believed to have been originally painted red. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} While stone human and horse figures are more commonly found in northern Kyushu, such as those from the [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], the Ishiumadani Kofun example is the only one found on Honshu. The stone’s origin indicates local craftsmanship but reflects cultural interactions between the San’in region and northern Kyushu. The stone horse was enshrined as "Stone Horse Daimyojin" during the [[Edo period]] and was first documented by Tsuboi Shōgorō in 1902. Further excavations during the Taisho period confirmed its connection to Ishiumadani Kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The artifact is now stored in the Tenjingaki Shrine repository. == Historic site == === [[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] (National) === * Stone Horse (archaeological artifact) - Originally located at Tenjingaki Shrine, Fukuoka, Yodoe Town, typeerly Saihaku District. Designated on December 18, 1959. {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}}[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] (Tottori Historic site Navigator).[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] (Yonago City Official Website). == References == {{references}} == Bibliography == {{small|Sources used for writing this article:}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/secure/16781/mukouyama.pdf Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet]}} (Yonago City Board of Education)|ref={{Harvid|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}}}} * {{Cite book|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige|year=1989|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofuns]]|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|year=1992|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Historical and Geographical Encyclopedia of Japan]]: Tottori Prefecture Volume|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} == External Links == * {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}} * [http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] - Tottori Historic site Navigator * [https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] - Yonago City Official Website in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category: of "[[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ancient tombs in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yonago City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Historic sites of Japan]] [[Category:Usuzuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[ja:臼塚古墳]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]] {{citation needed|ishiumadani kofun}} {{citation needed|Usuzuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{Kofun navbar}}

Kofun (front part to the left, rear circle to the back right) | alternate_name = Koeda-yama No. 5 Kofun {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} | part_of = [[Mukouyama Kofun Group]] (part of Koeda-yama Kofun Group) | location = [[Yonago City]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], Yodoe Town, Fukuoka | coordinates = 35|27|7.05|N | coordinates = 133|26|33.60|E | omitted = JP-31 | type = [[Keyhole-shaped tumulus]] | area = Length 61.2m
Height 5m (rear circle) | burial = Unknown | finds = Stone horse, [[Sue pottery]], [[Haniwa]] | satellites = | built = Mid [[6th century]] | Interred_individual = | tomb = | Historic site = National [[Historic site]] (part of the "Mukouyama Kofun Group") | tangible_notes = Stone horse (National [[Important designation1]]) | notable = Only example of [[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] on Honshu | map= {{location map|Japan Tottori|coordinates=35|coordinates=27|coordinates=7.05|lon_deg=133|lon_min=26|lon_sec=33.6|map_dot_label= Ishiumadani Kofun|mark=Keyhole.svg|marksize=23|caption=location in Tottori Prefecture|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|border=none}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|7.05|N|133|26|33.6|E}}}} }} '''Ishiumadani Kofun''' (石馬谷古墳, Koeda-yama No. 5 Kofun) is an ancient [[keyhole-shaped tumulus]] (zenpō-kōen-fun) located in [[Yonago City]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], Yodoe Town, Fukuoka. It is part of the [[Mukouyama Kofun Group]] and specifically belongs to the Koeda-yama Kofun Group. The tumulus has been Historic site as part of a National [[Historic site]], and a stone horse excavated from the site is a Historic site National [[Important designation1]]. The kofun is notable for being the only site on Honshu where [[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] (seijin-seiba) have been found. == Overview == The kofun is located on the slope of Koeda-yama Hills, in the eastern part of the Yodoe Plain in western Tottori Prefecture. It was partially excavated in [[1989]] (Heisei 1). {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The tumulus is keyhole-shaped, with its front part oriented southwest. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} Its total length measures 61.2 meters. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The surface of the mound features [[fukiishi]] (paving stones) as well as [[cylindrical haniwa]] (including morning-glory-shaped haniwa) and [[figurative haniwa]] (human-shaped and shield-shaped haniwa). {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The burial facility remains unknown. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} Other burial goods include a stone horse and [[Sue pottery]], with the stone horse being especially significant as the only such example found on Honshu. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The kofun is believed to have been built in the mid-[[6th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The site was Historic site as part of a National [[Historic site]] in [[1999]] (Heisei 11) under the "Mukouyama Kofun Group," and its stone horse was Historic site a National [[Important designation1]] in [[1959]] (Showa 34). == Chronology == * [[1902]] (Meiji 35): [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]] identified the stone horse at Tenjingaki Shrine. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} * During the [[Taisho period]] (1912–1926): Stone fragments were collected near the front part of the tumulus, and the stone horse at Tenjingaki Shrine was confirmed to have originated from this kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} * [[1959]] (Showa 34): The stone horse was Historic site a National [[Important designation1]]. * [[1989]] (Heisei 1): The site underwent trial excavation by the Yodoe Town Board of Education, and a report was published in [[1990]]. * [[1999]] (Heisei 11): The site was Historic site as part of the National [[Historic site]] "Mukouyama Kofun Group." {{National Historic site Database|401|2110|Mukouyama Kofun Group}} == Kofun == The area of the tumulus are as follows: {{PDFlink|[http://yonagobunka.net/system/site/upload/live/12551/atc_1522851322.pdf Yonago City Archaeological Center Newsletter No. 28]}} (Yonago City Archaeological Center, 2018). * Total length: 61.2 meters * Rear circle diameter: 34.5 meters * Rear circle height: 5 meters The tumulus is the second-largest in the Yodoe area, following Mukouyama No. 4 Kofun (64.5 meters). == burial goods == [[File:Ishiumadani Kofun Stone Horse (Replica).JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone horse (replica)}}{{small|Exhibited at the Hakuho Hill Museum. The original is a National [[Important designation1]].}}]] The most notable artifact from Ishiumadani Kofun is the '''stone horse'''. It is a stone representation of a haniwa horse and part of the broader Category:kk]]" (seijin-seiba). Although the front legs are missing, the surface shows detailed carvings of tack such as bridles, reins, and a sa11le. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The stone horse is believed to have been originally painted red. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} While stone human and horse figures are more commonly found in northern Kyushu, such as those from the [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], the Ishiumadani Kofun example is the only one found on Honshu. The stone’s origin indicates local craftsmanship but reflects cultural interactions between the San’in region and northern Kyushu. The stone horse was enshrined as "Stone Horse Daimyojin" during the [[Edo period]] and was first documented by Tsuboi Shōgorō in 1902. Further excavations during the Taisho period confirmed its connection to Ishiumadani Kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The artifact is now stored in the Tenjingaki Shrine repository. == Historic site == === [[Important designation1 (Japan)|Important designation1]] (National) === * Stone Horse (archaeological artifact) - Originally located at Tenjingaki Shrine, Fukuoka, Yodoe Town, typeerly Saihaku District. Designated on December 18, 1959. {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}}[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] (Tottori Historic site Navigator).[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] (Yonago City Official Website). == References == {{references}} == Bibliography == {{small|Sources used for writing this article:}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/secure/16781/mukouyama.pdf Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet]}} (Yonago City Board of Education)|ref={{Harvid|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}}}} * {{Cite book|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige|year=1989|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofuns]]|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|year=1992|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Historical and Geographical Encyclopedia of Japan]]: Tottori Prefecture Volume|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} == External Links == * {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}} * [http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] - Further excavations during the Taisho period confirmed its connection to Ishiumadani Kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The artifact is now stored in the Tenjingaki Shrine repository. == Historic site == === {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|ko|일본의_중요문화재}} (National) === * Stone Horse (archaeological artifact) - Originally located at Tenjingaki Shrine, Fukuoka, Yodoe Town, typeerly Saihaku District. Designated on December 18, 1959. {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}}[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] (Tottori Historic site Navigator).[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] (Yonago City Official Website). == References == {{references}} == Bibliography == {{small|Sources used for writing this article:}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/secure/16781/mukouyama.pdf Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet]}} (Yonago City Board of Education)|ref={{Harvid|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}}}} * {{Cite book|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige|year=1989|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofuns]]|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|year=1992|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Historical and Geographical Encyclopedia of Japan]]: Tottori Prefecture Volume|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} == External Links == * {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}} * [http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] - Tottori Historic site Navigator * [https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] - Yonago City Official Website in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category: of "[[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ancient tombs in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yonago City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Historic sites of Japan]] [[Category:Usuzuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[ja:臼塚古墳]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]] {{citation needed|ishiumadani kofun}} {{citation needed|Usuzuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Important designation1 (Japan)]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{Kofun navbar}}

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] | name = Ishiumadani Kofun | image = [[File:Ishiumadani Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Kofun (front part to the left, rear circle to the back right) | alternate_name = Koeda-yama No. 5 Kofun {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} | part_of = [[Mukouyama Kofun Group]] (part of Koeda-yama Kofun Group) | location = [[Yonago City]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], Yodoe Town, Fukuoka | coordinates = 35|27|7.05|N | coordinates = 133|26|33.60|E | omitted = JP-31 | type = [[Keyhole-shaped tumulus]] | area = Length 61.2m
Height 5m (rear circle) | burial = Unknown | finds = Stone horse, [[Sue pottery]], [[Haniwa]] | satellites = | built = Mid [[6th century]] | Interred_individual = | tomb = | Historic site = National [[Historic site]] (part of the "Mukouyama Kofun Group") | tangible_notes = Stone horse (National [[Important designation1]]) | notable = Only example of [[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] on Honshu | map= {{location map|Japan Tottori|coordinates=35|coordinates=27|coordinates=7.05|lon_deg=133|lon_min=26|lon_sec=33.6|map_dot_label= Ishiumadani Kofun|mark=Keyhole.svg|marksize=23|caption=location in Tottori Prefecture|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|border=none}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|27|7.05|N|133|26|33.6|E}}}} }} '''Ishiumadani Kofun''' (石馬谷古墳, Koeda-yama No. 5 Kofun) is an ancient [[keyhole-shaped tumulus]] (zenpō-kōen-fun) located in [[Yonago City]], [[Tottori Prefecture]], Yodoe Town, Fukuoka. It is part of the [[Mukouyama Kofun Group]] and specifically belongs to the Koeda-yama Kofun Group. The tumulus has been Historic site as part of a National [[Historic site]], and a stone horse excavated from the site is a Historic site National [[Important designation1]]. The kofun is notable for being the only site on Honshu where [[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] (seijin-seiba) have been found. == Overview == The kofun is located on the slope of Koeda-yama Hills, in the eastern part of the Yodoe Plain in western Tottori Prefecture. It was partially excavated in [[1989]] (Heisei 1). {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The tumulus is keyhole-shaped, with its front part oriented southwest. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} Its total length measures 61.2 meters. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The surface of the mound features [[fukiishi]] (paving stones) as well as [[cylindrical haniwa]] (including morning-glory-shaped haniwa) and [[figurative haniwa]] (human-shaped and shield-shaped haniwa). {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The burial facility remains unknown. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} Other burial goods include a stone horse and [[Sue pottery]], with the stone horse being especially significant as the only such example found on Honshu. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The kofun is believed to have been built in the mid-[[6th century]] during the late [[Kofun period]]. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The site was Historic site as part of a National [[Historic site]] in [[1999]] (Heisei 11) under the "Mukouyama Kofun Group," and its stone horse was Historic site a National [[Important designation1]] in [[1959]] (Showa 34). == Chronology == * [[1902]] (Meiji 35): [[Tsuboi Shōgorō]] identified the stone horse at Tenjingaki Shrine. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}} * During the [[Taisho period]] (1912–1926): Stone fragments were collected near the front part of the tumulus, and the stone horse at Tenjingaki Shrine was confirmed to have originated from this kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} * [[1959]] (Showa 34): The stone horse was Historic site a National [[Important designation1]]. * [[1989]] (Heisei 1): The site underwent trial excavation by the Yodoe Town Board of Education, and a report was published in [[1990]]. * [[1999]] (Heisei 11): The site was Historic site as part of the National [[Historic site]] "Mukouyama Kofun Group." {{National Historic site Database|401|2110|Mukouyama Kofun Group}} == Kofun == The area of the tumulus are as follows: {{PDFlink|[http://yonagobunka.net/system/site/upload/live/12551/atc_1522851322.pdf Yonago City Archaeological Center Newsletter No. 28]}} (Yonago City Archaeological Center, 2018). * Total length: 61.2 meters * Rear circle diameter: 34.5 meters * Rear circle height: 5 meters The tumulus is the second-largest in the Yodoe area, following Mukouyama No. 4 Kofun (64.5 meters). == burial goods == [[File:Ishiumadani Kofun Stone Horse (Replica).JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Stone horse (replica)}}{{small|Exhibited at the Hakuho Hill Museum. The original is a National [[Important designation1]].}}]] The most notable artifact from Ishiumadani Kofun is the '''stone horse'''. It is a stone representation of a haniwa horse and part of the broader Category:kk]]" (seijin-seiba). The stone, identified as hornblende [[andesite]] from Mount Daisen, measures approximately 150 cm in length and 90 cm in height. Intypeational plaque at Tenjingaki Shrine (Tottori Prefecture Board of Education, 2015). Although the front legs are missing, the surface shows detailed carvings of tack such as bridles, reins, and a sa11le. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} The stone horse is believed to have been originally painted red. {{Sfn|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}} While stone human and horse figures are more commonly found in northern Kyushu, such as those from the [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Fukuoka Prefecture]], the Ishiumadani Kofun example is the only one found on Honshu. The stone’s origin indicates local craftsmanship but reflects cultural interactions between the San’in region and northern Kyushu. The stone horse was enshrined as "Stone Horse Daimyojin" during the [[Edo period]] and was first documented by Tsuboi Shōgorō in 1902. Further excavations during the Taisho period confirmed its connection to Ishiumadani Kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The artifact is now stored in the Tenjingaki Shrine repository. == Historic site == === {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|ko|일본의_중요문화재}} (National) === * Stone Horse (archaeological artifact) - Originally located at Tenjingaki Shrine, Fukuoka, Yodoe Town, typeerly Saihaku District. Designated on December 18, 1959. {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}}[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] (Tottori Historic site Navigator).[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] (Yonago City Official Website). == References == {{references}} == Bibliography == {{small|Sources used for writing this article:}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/secure/16781/mukouyama.pdf Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet]}} (Yonago City Board of Education)|ref={{Harvid|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}}}} * {{Cite book|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige|year=1989|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofuns]]|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|year=1992|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Historical and Geographical Encyclopedia of Japan]]: Tottori Prefecture Volume|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} == External Links == * {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}} * [http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] - Tottori Historic site Navigator * [https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] - Yonago City Official Website in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category: of "[[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ancient tombs in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yonago City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Historic sites of Japan]] [[Category:Usuzuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[ja:臼塚古墳]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]] {{citation needed|ishiumadani kofun}} {{citation needed|Usuzuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Important designation1 (Japan)]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{Kofun navbar}}

Further excavations during the Taisho period confirmed its connection to Ishiumadani Kofun. {{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}{{Sfn|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}} The artifact is now stored in the Tenjingaki Shrine repository. == Historic site == === {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|ko|일본의_중요문화재}} (National) === * Stone Horse (archaeological artifact) - Originally located at Tenjingaki Shrine, Fukuoka, Yodoe Town, typeerly Saihaku District. Designated on December 18, 1959. {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}}[http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] (Tottori Historic site Navigator).[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] (Yonago City Official Website). == References == {{references}} == Bibliography == {{small|Sources used for writing this article:}} * {{Wikicite|reference={{PDFlink|[https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/secure/16781/mukouyama.pdf Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet]}} (Yonago City Board of Education)|ref={{Harvid|Mukouyama Kofun Group Pamphlet}}}} * {{Cite book|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige|year=1989|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Encyclopedia of Japanese Kofuns]]|publisher=Tokyo-dō Publishing|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|year=1992|chapter=Ishiumadani Kofun|title=[[Historical and Geographical Encyclopedia of Japan]]: Tottori Prefecture Volume|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582490328|ref={{Harvid|Ishiumadani Kofun (Heibonsha)|1992}}}} == External Links == * {{National Historic site Database|201|9815|Stone Horse}} * [http://db.pref.tottori.jp/bunkazainavi.nsf/bunkazai_web_view/7DCBB322DC32342A4925796F0007FC82?OpenDocument Stone Horse] - Tottori Historic site Navigator * [https://www.city.yonago.lg.jp/16703.htm Stone Horse] - Yonago City Official Website in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category: of "[[Sekijin sekiba|stone human and horse figures]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ancient tombs in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yonago City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Historic sites of Japan]] [[Category:Usuzuka Kofun]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[ja:臼塚古墳]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]] {{citation needed|ishiumadani kofun}} {{citation needed|Usuzuka Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]][[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]][[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] [[sn:Usuzuka Kofun]]{{Kofun navbar}}

revision.text:
```wikitext {{Infobox Japanese tumulus | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片}}<!--cat=臼杵市--> | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|zh|前方后圆坟}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.
== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表}} → Ōita Prefecture section.

[[ja:臼塚古墳]] [[de:Usuzuka Kofun]] ```

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.
== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表}} → Ōita Prefecture section.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=See [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]]
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片}}<!--cat=臼杵市--> | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.

Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.

With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

{{afc comment|1=See [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]][[Category:Secondary Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]][[Category:Secondary Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]][[Category:Secondary Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]]
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片}}<!--cat=臼杵市--> | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.
== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表}} → Ōita Prefecture section.

Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

revision.comment: 16 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:臼塚古墳 up to rev 96036217
revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称=臼塚古墳 |別名= |所属= |画像={{画像募集中|cat=臼杵市}} |所在地=大分県臼杵市大字稲田字林、西平 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|8|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000}} |形状=[[前方後円墳]] |規模= |築造年代= |出土品= |史跡指定= |埋葬施設= |地図2= {{Location map|Japan Oita|lat_deg=33|lat_min=8|lat_sec=37.1|lon_deg=131|lon_min=47|lon_sec=11|label=臼塚古墳|mark=前方後円墳-南.svg|marksize=23|caption=大分県内の位置|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|border=none}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|33|8|37.1|N|131|47|11|E}}}} }}
'''臼塚古墳'''（うすづかこふん）は、[[大分県]][[臼杵市]]大字稲田にある[[古墳]]。1948年9月10日に大分県の[[史跡]]に指定されている。
== 概要 == 臼杵市北部を流れる熊崎川流域の[[洪積台地]]（通称、三重野台地）の標高約20mの台地南端にある、全長約87m、後円部径約45m、高さ約6m、前方部幅約45mの臼杵地方最大の[[前方後円墳]]である。[[5世紀]]中頃の築造と推定されている。
墳丘上には[[明治時代]]に[[臼杵神社]]の現在の社殿が建築されており、このために墳丘の一部が切削されているが、全体としては原形をよくとどめている。前方部と後円部の境には、元来2基の[[石人石馬|石甲]]（いずれも高さ135cm）が立てられていた。これらの石甲は1976年6月5日に国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されており、現在は臼杵神社境内の参道脇に覆屋を設けて移設されている。
臼杵神社の現在の社殿建築時に、後円部から大小2基の舟形石棺（全長285cm及び225cm）が発見されており、各石棺は、一枚岩をくりぬいて造られ、内面全体に朱が施されており、それぞれ2体の人骨が埋葬されていたとしている<ref>大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告第6号』</ref>。
これらの人骨の[[外耳]]には潜水によって生じることが多い外耳道骨腫が認められることから、被葬者は[[海人]]であったと推定されている。また、副葬品として、位至三公双龍鏡（直径9.9cm）及び変形獣帯鏡（直径9.7cm）の2面の鏡、鉄剣、鉄刀、鉄鉾、短甲等の武具や、[[貝輪]]、[[勾玉]]等の装身具が出土している。なお、鏡はいずれも伯載鏡である。
被葬者は、古墳の規模、副葬品、外耳の特徴から、この地域の海人の支配者層であると考えられている。
== 石甲 == 臼塚古墳に見られる石甲は、[[石人石馬]]のような石像の一種で、細い円柱状の台部に、2つの円錐を頂部でつなげたような形状の胴部が載る石製品であり、甲冑を着た人を象った石像であるとされる。ただし、頭部は作られていない。その形が臼と杵のようにも見えることから、臼杵の地名はこの石甲から起こったものであるとする説もある<ref>{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/eki008a1.pdf 各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見]}} 梅木秀徳、大分合同新聞社、2007年4月20日</ref>。
全国的に見ると、石人、石馬は、北部九州を中心に分布し、そのうち特に[[筑紫君磐井]]の墓に比定される福岡県の[[岩戸山古墳]]では、80基を超える石人、石馬が発見されている。大分県内では他に、同じく臼杵市の熊崎川流域にある[[下山古墳 (臼杵市)|下山古墳]]のみで見られるもので、北部九州の分布域から離れた[[豊後水道]]沿岸の臼杵にのみ分布していることが注目されている。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ 臼塚古墳] - 臼杵市HP

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片}}<!--cat=臼杵市--> | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.

{{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|qq=}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki, Ōita|qq=}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōen-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|kofun|de|Kofun|ja|古墳|zh|古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|qq=}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|de|Usuki|ja|臼杵市|zh|臼杵市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki, Ōita|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|de|Präfektur Ōita|ja|大分県|zh|大分县|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|de|Historische Stätte (Japan)|ja|史跡|zh|史迹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historic Site (Japan)|qq=}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|de|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|zh|前方后圆坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō‑kōen‑fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|de|Wichtiges Kulturgut Japans|ja|重要文化財|zh|日本重要文化财|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|ama (diver)|de|Ama (Taucher)|ja|海人|zh|海人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ama (diver)|qq=}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|qq=}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|qq=}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|magatama|de|Magatama|ja|勾玉|zh|曲玉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/magatama|qq=}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|stone figures of kofun|de|Steinfiguren japanischer Kofun|ja|石人石馬|1=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba|zh|石人石马|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone figures of kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekijin sekiba|qq=}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|de|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|ja|下山古墳 (臼杵市)|zh|下山古坟 (臼杵市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|qq=}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|qq=}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|qq=}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]] [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]] Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.
== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}} → Ōita Prefecture section.
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳)
