{{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{coord|33|08|37.1|N|131|47|11.0|E|scale:10000|display=inline,title|region:JP-44_type:landmark}} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]] [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]] Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.

== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}} → Ōita Prefecture section.

== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{Coord|33.143222|N|131.785917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]] [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]] Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic stone coffins | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=|1=Usuki-jinja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped stone coffins were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|qq=|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.

Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:臼塚古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11613253]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{Coord|33.143222|N|131.785917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]] [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]] Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic Sarcophaguss | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{Coord|33.143222|N|131.785917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]] [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]] Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic Sarcophaguss | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large [[:en:Kofun|kofun]] burial mound in Inada, [[:en:Usuki, Ōita|Usuki, Ōita]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]], Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural [[:en:Historic site|Historic Site (Japan)]].
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property (Japan)]] on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite [[:en:Ama (diving)|ama (diver)]] seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, [[:en:Magatama|magatama]], and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of [[:en:Sekijin sekiba|Sekijin sekiba]]. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby [[:en:Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)]], making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} in the Usuki area. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|Ama (diving)|en|Ama (diving)|WD=Q1577970|lt=ama (diver)}} seafarers.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{Coord|33.143222|N|131.785917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic Sarcophaguss | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=kofun}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic Site (Japan)}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|Ama (diving)|en|Ama (diving)|WD=Q1577970|lt=ama (diver)}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|Magatama|en|Magatama|WD=Q334232|lt=magatama}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|Sekijin sekiba|en|Sekijin sekiba|WD=Q17994947|lt=Sekijin sekiba}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|en|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|WD=Q11360813|lt=Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:臼塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:臼塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:臼塚古墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{Coord|33.143222|N|131.785917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole‑shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid‑5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat‑shaped hollowed monolithic Sarcophaguss | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=kofun}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic Site (Japan)}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid‑5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat‑shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|Ama (diving)|en|Ama (diving)|WD=Q1577970|lt=ama (diver)}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei‑zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei‑zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast‑band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai‑kyō|de|Hakusai‑kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai‑kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|Magatama|en|Magatama|WD=Q334232|lt=magatama}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|Sekijin sekiba|en|Sekijin sekiba|WD=Q17994947|lt=Sekijin sekiba}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double‑cone torso, interpreted as an armour‑clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place‑name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|en|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|WD=Q11360813|lt=Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.
== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}} → Ōita Prefecture section.

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Usuzuka Kofun | native_name = 臼塚古墳 | image = {{ill|Image requested|de|Bild fehlt|ja|画像募集中|zh|缺少图片|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Image requested|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | map_type = Japan Oita Prefecture#Japan | relief = yes | coordinates = {{Coord|33.143222|N|131.785917|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}} {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita|1=Usuki, Ōita}} Inada, Hayashi / Nishidaira | type = Keyhole-shaped tumulus ({{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}) | length = ≈ 87 m | diameter = Rear mound ≈ 45 m | width = Front mound ≈ 45 m | height = ≈ 6 m (rear mound) | built = mid-5th century CE (Kofun period) | excavation = – | burial_chamber= Two boat-shaped hollowed monolithic Sarcophaguss | built_by = Regional maritime elite | public_access = yes }}
'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=kofun|1=Kofun}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita|1=Usuki, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic Site (Japan)|1=Historic site}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid-5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat-shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|Ama (diving)|en|Ama (diving)|WD=Q1577970|lt=ama (diver)|1=Ama (diving)}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei-zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei-zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei-zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast-band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai-kyō|de|Hakusai-kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|Magatama|en|Magatama|WD=Q334232|lt=magatama|1=Magatama}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|Sekijin sekiba|en|Sekijin sekiba|WD=Q17994947|lt=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double-cone torso, interpreted as an armour-clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place-name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|en|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|WD=Q11360813|lt=Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|1=Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.

== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.

'''Usuzuka Kofun''' (臼塚古墳) is a large {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=kofun|1=Kofun}} burial mound in Inada, {{ill|Usuki, Ōita|en|Usuki, Ōita|WD=Q845792|lt=Usuki, Ōita|1=Usuki, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, Japan. On 10 September 1948 it was designated a prefectural {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic Site (Japan)|1=Historic site}}.
== Overview == The mound stands on the southern edge of the Miyeno Plateau (a Pleistocene terrace) along the Kumazaki River in northern Usuki, at c. 20 m a.s.l. With an overall length of about 87 m, a rear circle 45 m in diameter and 6 m high, and a front width of 45 m, it is the largest {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} in the Usuki area. Construction is dated to the mid-5th century CE.
The present {{ill|Usuki Shrine|de|Usuki-jinja|ja|臼杵神社|zh|臼杵神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Shrine|1=Usuki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11613283}} was built atop the mound in the Meiji era, shaving part of the tumulus, yet the overall shape remains well preserved. At the junction of the front and rear sections originally stood two stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'', each 135 cm high). Both were designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} on 5 June 1976 and are now sheltered beside the shrine approach.
During construction of the shrine buildings two hollowed monolithic boat-shaped Sarcophaguss were found in the rear mound (lengths 285 cm and 225 cm). The interiors were fully coated with cinnabar and each contained two human skeletons. The external ear canals of the skulls show exostoses typical of habitual divers, suggesting that the interred were elite {{ill|Ama (diving)|en|Ama (diving)|WD=Q1577970|lt=ama (diver)|1=Ama (diving)}} seafarers.
Grave goods comprise two bronze mirrors – a “{{ill|Wei-zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|de|Wei-zhi Sangong Shuanglong Spiegel|ja|位至三公双龍鏡|zh|位至三公双龙镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wei-zhi sangong shuanglong mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (Ø 9.9 cm) and a variant beast-band mirror (Ø 9.7 cm) – both rimless ({{ill|hakusai-kyō|de|Hakusai-kyō|ja|伯載鏡|zh|伯载镜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakusai-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), together with iron swords, spears, daggers, armour plates, shell bangles, {{ill|Magatama|en|Magatama|WD=Q334232|lt=magatama|1=Magatama}}, and other ornaments.
Taken together, mound size, grave goods, and pathological ear lesions indicate that the occupants belonged to the ruling class of a maritime community controlling local coastal trade and fisheries.
== Stone cuirass figures (''sekikō'') == The Usuzuka stone cuirasses are a regional variant of {{ill|Sekijin sekiba|en|Sekijin sekiba|WD=Q17994947|lt=Sekijin sekiba|1=Sekijin sekiba}}. Each consists of a slender cylindrical base supporting a double-cone torso, interpreted as an armour-clad human without a head. Because their silhouette resembles a mortar and pestle (''usu'' and ''kine''), one theory derives the place-name ''Usuki'' from these figures.citeturn0source1
Nationally, stone figures cluster in northern Kyūshū; the {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|de|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|zh|岩户山古坟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}} (thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Prince Iwai|de|Iwai (König von Tsukushi)|ja|筑紫君磐井|3=磐井 (古代豪族)|1=Tsukushi no Iwai|2=ja|zh|筑紫君磐井|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Iwai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukushi no Iwai|qq=}}) yielded over 80 specimens. Within Ōita Prefecture they appear only here and at the nearby {{ill|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|en|Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|WD=Q11360813|lt=Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)|1=Shimoyama Kofun (Usuki)}}, making their distribution along the Bungo Strait–Usuki coast noteworthy.
== Notes == {{Notelist|30em}}
== References == {{Reflist|18em}} * 大分県史蹟名勝天然記念物調査会『史蹟名勝天然記念物調査報告 第6号』. * 梅木秀徳「各駅停車・大分県歴史散歩 ふるさとの駅 (8) 臼杵・津久見」『大分合同新聞』20 Apr 2007. PDF.
== External links == * [http://www.city.usuki.oita.jp/docs/2014020500356/ Usuzuka Kofun] – Usuki City official site.
== See also == * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|de|Liste der Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|zh|日本古坟列表|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}} → Ōita Prefecture section.
